old_claim.note: Operating Systems (OS Computer Management) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Operating-system shell and window-management services supplied a common platform for WIMP-style GUIs; this is enabling platform context, not a requirement for every experimental GUI lineage.

source_shape.source_locator: Historical overview covers WIMP-style graphical interaction and its adoption through Xerox, Apple, and Microsoft operating environments.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source describes GUIs as computer interaction environments that were operationalized through workstation and personal-computer operating environments.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports operating systems as the platform layer for mainstream GUIs while preserving the edge as enabling context because early research GUIs could also be built in narrower experimental environments.

invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: graphical_user_interface_gui keeps operating_systems_os_computer_management as an enabling platform edge with a narrowed WIMP/window-manager rationale.
would_reject_if: The GUI node were narrowed to pre-operating-system interactive graphics experiments only.
A source showed mainstream WIMP GUIs developed independently of operating-system shell or window-management services.
The operating_systems_os_computer_management node were narrowed to batch-only monitor software without interactive services.
short_reason: Operating environments supplied the platform layer for mainstream WIMP GUIs.
